nativeRange.habitat: Found among coral and live rock on reef slopes and walls, typically at depths between 5 and 21 metres.

diet.notes: Has been observed accepting a wide variety of aquarium foods; tends to thrive when offered finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and frozen herbivore preparations. Multiple feedings per day are generally recommended. ORA captive-bred specimens have been reported to readily consume all types of aquarium foods.

avoidWith: aggressive fish that may provoke defensive biting
multiple Meiacanthus blennies unless a bonded breeding pair

summary: The Bundoon Blenny is a small fang blenny native to the reefs of Fiji and Tonga, recognized by its dark body and a distinctive yellow-orange stripe running along the side. Generally considered reef-safe, it is best kept singly in a reef tank of 120 litres or more with plenty of live rock and hiding places.
careNotes: The Bundoon Blenny is a fang blenny (genus Meiacanthus) with venomous lower-jaw fangs used primarily for defense — distinct from spine-based venom. If provoked or attacked by tankmates, it may deliver a painful bite; extra caution is advised when handling or working in the tank. It is not aggressive toward invertebrates or corals and is generally considered reef safe. Best kept as a single specimen; a male-female breeding pair can be housed together in a larger tank. Males tend to be larger and may display intensified coloration during courtship. The species is generally considered hardy and durable in captive conditions. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available and have been reported to acclimate readily to prepared aquarium foods. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended as blennies of this genus are known to jump. Named after the yacht Bundoon, aboard which it was discovered by Dr. Bruce Carlson in Fiji in 1974.
breedingNotes: The Bundoon Blenny is an egg-laying species that forms distinct male-female pairs for spawning. The eggs are small and adhesive, attached directly to a hard surface rather than scattered into open water. Males engage in courtship displays when kept with other fish of the same species. Captive breeding has been achieved commercially, and captive-bred specimens are available through the aquarium trade.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (9.5 cm SL), temperature (25.2–27.6°C), native range (Fiji and Tonga), and taxonomy. FishBase temperature skews higher than LiveAquaria; final temperature range was averaged across three sources.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.1 in / 7.9 cm), temperature (75–82°F / 23.9–27.8°C), lifespan (4–6 years), temperament, and compatibility details. Size and temperature used for averaging.

additional.name: ORA Farm
additional.notes: Confirmed captive-bred availability, feeding acceptance, and courtship behavior in groups.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been accomplished commercially; eggs are small, adhesive, and attach to substrate, with larvae passing through a planktonic phase before settling. Breeding in home aquariums is uncommon.
